claims.text: In 1793, Secretary of State Thomas Jefferson - a Francophile sympathetic to revolutionary France's new decimal-based weights and measures - dispatched French scientist Joseph Dombey to Philadelphia carrying the prototype kilogram weight ('grave') and meter scale that would have made the United States a metric country from inception.

claims.rationale: Joseph Dombey (rendered as 'Dumais' in transcript) was a real French botanist sent in 1793 with the metric standards for Jefferson. Jefferson was indeed Secretary of State and a documented Francophile. The historical record confirms the dispatch and the fact that the French Revolution was actively rolling out the metric system at the time.

claims.text: Dombey's metric-standards ship was blown off course into the Caribbean where British privateers (legally-sanctioned pirates) intercepted it, captured Dombey, threw him in prison on Montserrat where he died, and auctioned off the prototype kilogram and meter - meaning the very objects that would have anchored America to the metric system were sold to scrap.

claims.rationale: Joseph Dombey was indeed captured by British privateers in 1794, imprisoned on Montserrat (the host's 'Almanzora' is a transcription artifact), and died in captivity. The metric standards he was carrying were lost. This is real - it's regularly cited as one of those historical 'what if' moments that almost converted the US to metric.

claims.text: Congress authorized the metric system in 1866 and supplied each state with metric weights and measures, but Americans rejected it because the US customary system (based on body parts: a foot is foot-length, an inch a thumb-width) felt more tangible and immediate than abstract decimal units.

claims.rationale: The Metric Act of 1866 is real - it legalized but did not require metric in the US. The 'tangible body-part units' argument for customary measure is a common rhetorical defense, though scholars dispute that human-scale intuitiveness actually outweighs metric's computational simplicity. Customary units have actually shifted over time (the modern inch is defined as exactly 25.4mm), so 'consistent' is a stretch.

claims.text: Despite the US signing the international Meter Convention in 1875, American industrialists successfully argued that switching would be too disruptive to existing infrastructure - and as a compromise, since 1893 every US customary unit (yard, pound, inch, mile) has been *officially defined* as a fixed conversion of its metric equivalent, making American measurements covertly metric.

claims.rationale: The 1875 Meter Convention (Treaty of the Meter) is real and the US was a signatory. The Mendenhall Order of 1893 did make metric the fundamental US standard, with customary units derived from metric equivalents. Today the international yard is defined as exactly 0.9144 meters and the international pound as exactly 0.45359237 kilograms - so the host's punchline that customary units are technically metric is correct.

claims.text: Congress recommended the US go metric within 10 years in 1971 and passed the Metric Conversion Act of 1975, but the act was voluntary and never required adoption - so most consumers stuck with customary units while big firms (Xerox, GM, IBM, Ford) quietly switched on their own to capture cost savings on international markets.

claims.rationale: Both the 1971 NBS report 'A Metric America' and the 1975 Metric Conversion Act are real, and the act was indeed voluntary - explicitly so. Big US manufacturers genuinely did switch to metric internally for global supply-chain reasons; the auto industry in particular was largely metric by the 1980s.

claims.text: NASA's $125 million Mars Climate Orbiter crashed into Mars in 1999 because Lockheed Martin engineers gave thruster impulse data in pound-force-seconds while NASA's navigation team assumed the values were in metric Newton-seconds - a unit-conversion failure that demolished years of work and became the canonical case study for why mixing systems is dangerous.

claims.rationale: This is one of the most famous engineering failures of the 20th century. The Mars Climate Orbiter was lost on September 23, 1999 because Lockheed Martin's ground software output thruster impulse in pound-force-seconds while NASA's spacecraft software expected newton-seconds. The Mishap Investigation Board's report is public. Mission cost was about $327M total but the orbiter alone was ~$125M. The host's framing is correct.

claims.text: The host's tongue-in-cheek narrative casts former Rhode Island Governor Lincoln Chafee's failed 2016 presidential bid - which made adopting the metric system a core campaign plank - as evidence that no political movement to make America metric can succeed even when it nominally has a candidate.

claims.rationale: Lincoln Chafee really did make adopting the metric system a campaign theme during his short-lived 2015-2016 Democratic presidential primary run. He withdrew in October 2015 after one debate. His metric pitch became a minor punchline in political coverage at the time. The host's framing is essentially correct.

claims.text: In the host's verdict, the United States is already a metric country in practice: all federal agencies are required to use metric, all consumer and food products must carry metric labels, and manufacturing increasingly uses metric - the cultural attachment to customary units is largely a consumer-facing veneer over an underlying metric reality.

claims.rationale: The Metric Conversion Act as amended (1988) really does require federal agencies to use metric for procurement and grants where economical. The Fair Packaging and Labeling Act mandates dual metric/customary labels on most consumer products. American manufacturing is heavily metric for international trade reasons. The host's claim is essentially accurate - the US is far more metric than its everyday culture suggests.
